w:r.w:t: Every visible element in the app, what it means, and how it is computed.
A unitless index of how much the scenario reduces storm runoff potential relative to fully paved land. Higher is better.
is the area-weighted average SCS Curve Number across all pixels.
Not a direct percentage reduction in runoff volume — it is a CN-derived index. Green infrastructure (NLCD 90) drives the largest improvements; high-density development (NLCD 24) makes it worse.
Approximate air-temperature difference vs the unmodified baseline, expressed as a direction and magnitude (e.g. “1.2°F cooler” or “0.8°F warmer”).

per InVEST Urban Cooling Model. Calibration: 4.0°F/HM is an approximate midpoint from InVEST UCM literature (range 2–5°C per HM unit); not locally calibrated for Minneapolis. Treat as directional only.
(shade and kc columns per NLCD lucode).
Raw HM values for the scenario and baseline shown side-by-side (e.g. “HM 0.3142 vs 0.2719”).
Calibration factor of 4 °F/HM unit is approximate for Minneapolis; accuracy is roughly ±2 °F. The HM index is a proxy, not a direct temperature measurement.

The absolute stormwater runoff volume the scenario would generate for a 2-inch design storm, in acre-feet. This is the scenario’s total runoff, not a reduction from baseline — lower values mean the scenario retains more water, but the number itself is not a “prevented” delta. The delta vs baseline is shown in the card’s sub-label and is computed explicitly in the “Cost / Acre-Foot Prevented” ratio.
(see Flood Risk Reduction) and total developed acreage (
A 2-inch storm represents a common minor design event; results for larger storms will differ. The card’s delta sub-label shows the explicit reduction vs baseline (green) or increase (red).
Annual food yield from pixels converted to food forest land cover, in millions of pounds per year.
Pixel count from the scenario LULC raster (NLCD code 41 pixels). Yield benchmark from San Antonio NatCap study.

11,500 lbs/acre/year is a benchmark estimate, not site-specific. NLCD code 41 (deciduous forest) is used as a proxy for food-producing tree cover. Actual yield depends on species mix, management, and soil quality.
Rough order-of-magnitude total capital cost to implement all conversions in the scenario.
Pixel counts from the scenario LULC; per-acre costs from the sidebar sliders.
Does not include maintenance, land acquisition, or displacement costs. Defaults ($50k/acre GI, $10k/acre FF, $5k/acre HD) are rough midpoints; use the sliders to reflect local conditions.
when the denominator is zero or negative (no improvement vs baseline) or when total cost is zero (no conversions).
Implementation dollars spent per acre-foot of runoff reduction vs the unmodified baseline.
N/A if the scenario increases runoff (e.g. all high-density). Lower is better.
Implementation dollars spent per degree Fahrenheit of cooling vs baseline.
N/A if the scenario is warmer than baseline. Inherits the ±2 °F uncertainty of the HM-to-temperature calibration.
Implementation dollars spent per 1,000 people whose annual food needs could be met by the scenario’s food forest yield.
N/A if no food forest pixels are present. Inherits the food yield benchmark uncertainty.

A prose sentence rendered below the metric cards: “This scenario converts X% of developed land, allocating Y% to green infrastructure, Z% to food forest, and W% to high-density development, [using random placement / prioritizing hotter areas].”
Pulled directly from the current slider values (pct_converted, green_infrastructure_pct, food_forest_pct, pct_highdensity).
“prioritizing hotter areas” when the heat-priority toggle is on; “using random placement” otherwise.
dict; unavailable cities are labelled “(coming soon)”.
Selecting an unavailable city shows a warning and halts execution via
. Selecting an available city sets data paths and baseline constants for all downstream computation.
Determines how many developed pixels (NLCD 21/22/23) are randomly sampled for conversion. At 50%, half of all developed pixels are converted.
Pixels are chosen randomly (or heat-priority weighted); spatial placement is not optimised for corridors or parcels.
Green Infrastructure % / Food Forest % / High Density %
Number inputs, range 0–100, step 5; must sum to exactly 100
Splits the converted pixels into three groups. Green Infrastructure → NLCD 90 (woody wetlands). Food Forest → NLCD 41 (deciduous forest proxy). High Density → NLCD 24 (developed, high intensity).
High Density auto-fills as the remainder but can be manually overridden. The app shows a warning and stops if the three values do not sum to 100.
Scales the implementation cost calculation. Does not affect ecological outcomes (CN, HM, food).
The lookup table is pre-computed at default costs; cost is recomputed from pixel counts and current slider values on every interaction.
Toggle, default off; under subheader “Spatial Priority”

When on, converted pixels are sampled with probability weights: NLCD 23 → 1.0, NLCD 22 → 0.6, NLCD 21 → 0.3. Concentrates interventions in higher-intensity developed areas. Negative or NaN weights are clamped to 0 before normalisation.

This is a land-use intensity proxy, not a measured heat or socioeconomic vulnerability index. The lookup table (which uses uniform random placement) is bypassed in this mode; scenarios are computed live. Intended as a placeholder until CDC/ATSDR HVI data is integrated.
Slider 0–90, step 5. Minimum acceptable Flood Risk Reduction index.

Slider −1.0 to ~3.0°F, step 0.1, default 0.1°F. Minimum acceptable temperature improvement vs baseline. Converted to HM units internally via BASELINE_HM + min_cool_f / 4.0 before being passed to the surrogate.
Slider 0.0–MAX_FOOD, step 0.01. Minimum acceptable food production.

Samples ~10,000 random (pct, GI%, FF%) combinations, predicts outcomes with the RF surrogate, filters to those meeting all three minimums, computes the Pareto front, de-duplicates near-identical points, and returns up to 5 top suggestions ranked by a balanced score:
Surrogate predictions carry uncertainty (shown as 10th–90th percentile bands). Cost and heat-priority mode are not inputs to the surrogate — apply those after selecting a suggestion.
Built with Plotly. X and Y axes show the two primary ecological metrics; bubble size encodes food production for saved and optimised scenarios only.
Mean HM across all pixels. Range fixed 0–1.1. Higher = better (more cooling potential). Note: this is the raw HM value, not the °F delta shown in the metric card.
Four fixed points representing extreme all-one-landcover scenarios for Minneapolis. Hardcoded in
. These values are specific to Minneapolis 2021 NLCD data and will differ for other cities — each city added to the CITIES dict must supply its own benchmark values.
The boundary of all outcomes reachable across the pre-computed scenario grid and lookup table.
on (flood_reduction, mean_HM) coordinates of all pre-computed scenarios.
The hull is over the discrete grid, not a continuous surface — scenarios near the hull boundary are achievable but the interior is not uniformly dense.
The outcome of the current slider settings. Crosshair lines extend from the star to both axes for easier reading.
Scenarios explicitly saved by clicking “Save this scenario.” Bubble size scales with food production (
). Hovering shows the scenario name and all three metric values.
The subset of saved scenarios where no other saved scenario is strictly better on all three metrics simultaneously (flood, HM, food).
Iterative dominance check: a point is Pareto-efficient if no other point dominates it on all three dimensions.
Computed over saved scenarios only; adding more saved scenarios can change which points are on the frontier.
Returned by the surrogate optimizer after clicking “Optimize.” Error bars show the 10th–90th percentile range across Random Forest trees for flood (horizontal) and HM (vertical).
Shown below the optimised suggestions table when optimizer results are present.
Which of the three input sliders (% Converted, Green Infra %, Food Forest %) most strongly drives the surrogate model’s predictions across all three output metrics.
model.feature_importances_ from the Random Forest — the mean decrease in impurity averaged across all trees and all output dimensions.
Importances reflect the surrogate’s learned relationships, not physical causation. Because the three inputs are constrained (GI% + FF% ≤ 100%), their importances are not fully independent.
Three side-by-side bars comparing the current scenario to the unmodified baseline on the three primary metrics.
A horizontal dashed line at the baseline value is drawn on the Flood Risk and Cooling charts for visual reference. The Food Production y-axis is scaled to
Pixel-level raster showing which developed pixels changed and to what land cover.
When heat-priority mode is on, teal/green/red pixels are concentrated in higher-intensity developed areas rather than uniformly distributed.
